questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Ecosystems are described as open systems because:

options.text: Both energy and matter can enter and exit
Only energy can enter and exit
Only matter can enter and exit

questions.explanation: Ecosystems are open systems: both energy and matter can enter and exit. In closed systems only energy passes in and out.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: What is the principal source of energy that sustains most ecosystems?
questions.explanation: Sunlight is the principal energy source for most ecosystems; exceptions include caves and the deep ocean.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Which ecosystem is an exception to sunlight being the principal energy source?

options.text: Deep-ocean ecosystems below the level of light penetration
A coral reef in shallow water

questions.explanation: Ecosystems in caves and below the level of light penetration in oceans are exceptions to sunlight-based energy.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: What passes from the leaf to the caterpillar?
questions.explanation: Chemical energy passes to a consumer as it feeds on the previous stage of a food chain.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: In a food chain diagram, arrows are drawn between organisms.
questions.stem: What does the direction of each arrow show?

options.text: The direction of transfer of energy and biomass

questions.explanation: In food chains and webs, arrows indicate the direction of transfer of energy and biomass.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Decomposers obtain energy from:

options.text: Carbon compounds in dead organisms, faeces and dead parts

questions.explanation: Decomposers get energy from organic matter: faeces, dead parts of organisms and dead whole organisms.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Autotrophs are organisms that:

options.text: Synthesize carbon compounds from inorganic substances using an external energy source
Obtain carbon compounds only from other organisms

questions.explanation: Autotrophs use external energy to synthesize carbon compounds from simple inorganic substances.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Photoautotrophs use which external energy source?
questions.explanation: Photoautotrophs use light as the external energy source; chemoautotrophs use oxidation reactions.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: Iron-oxidizing bacteria obtain energy from chemical reactions.
questions.stem: These bacteria are:
questions.explanation: Chemoautotrophs such as iron-oxidizing bacteria use oxidation reactions as their energy source.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Heterotrophs obtain carbon compounds by:

options.text: Making them from carbon dioxide using light

questions.explanation: Heterotrophs use carbon compounds obtained from other organisms to build the compounds they need.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Both autotrophs and heterotrophs release energy by:

options.text: Oxidation of carbon compounds in cell respiration

questions.explanation: Both autotrophs and heterotrophs release energy by oxidizing carbon compounds in cell respiration.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A plant makes its own food and is eaten by a rabbit.
questions.stem: The plant occupies which trophic level?
questions.explanation: Producers make their own food; primary consumers eat producers, and so on up the trophic levels.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A rabbit eats grass, and a fox eats the rabbit.
questions.stem: The fox is a:
questions.explanation: The grass is a producer, the rabbit a primary consumer and the fox a secondary consumer.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: An omnivore eats both plants and animals.
questions.stem: This shows that an organism can:

options.text: Occupy different trophic levels in different food chains

questions.explanation: Many organisms have varied diets and occupy different trophic levels in different food chains.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A diagram shows energy at each trophic level, with each bar smaller than the one below.
questions.stem: This diagram is a(n):
questions.explanation: An energy pyramid represents energy transfer and losses between trophic levels, narrowing at higher levels.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Why does the energy available decrease at each successive trophic level?

options.text: Large energy losses occur between trophic levels
Energy is created at each level

questions.explanation: Large energy losses between trophic levels reduce the energy available at each successive stage.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Decomposers and detritus feeders are usually:

options.text: Not considered part of food chains, though important in energy transformations
Always the top of the food chain

questions.explanation: Decomposers and detritus feeders are not usually placed in food chains but play a key role in energy transformations.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Heat is lost to the environment by organisms because:

options.text: Energy transfers in respiration are not 100% efficient

questions.explanation: Because energy transfers are not 100% efficient, heat is produced when ATP is made and used in cell respiration.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Why is the number of trophic levels in an ecosystem limited?

options.text: Energy losses mean too little energy remains for higher levels

questions.explanation: Energy losses between levels restrict the number of trophic levels, as insufficient energy reaches higher levels.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: At each successive trophic level there is generally:
questions.explanation: There are fewer or smaller organisms at higher levels, so there is less biomass (though energy per unit mass is not reduced).

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Primary production is the accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by:
questions.explanation: Primary production is the accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Which units are usually used for primary production?

options.text: Mass of carbon per unit area per unit time (g m-2 yr-1)

questions.explanation: Primary production is measured as mass of carbon per unit area per unit time, usually g m-2 yr-1.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Secondary production is the accumulation of biomass by:
questions.explanation: Secondary production is the accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by heterotrophs.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Compared with primary production, secondary production in an ecosystem is:
questions.explanation: Secondary production is lower than primary production because biomass is lost when carbon compounds are respired.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A carbon cycle diagram is drawn.
questions.stem: Which processes recycle carbon in ecosystems?
questions.explanation: Carbon is recycled in ecosystems by photosynthesis, feeding and respiration, shown in a carbon cycle diagram.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: In an ecosystem, photosynthesis exceeds respiration.
questions.stem: The ecosystem is acting as a:
questions.explanation: When photosynthesis exceeds respiration there is a net uptake of carbon dioxide, so the ecosystem is a carbon sink.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: In an ecosystem, respiration exceeds photosynthesis.
questions.stem: The ecosystem is acting as a:
questions.explanation: When respiration exceeds photosynthesis there is a net release of carbon dioxide, so the ecosystem is a carbon source.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Which process releases carbon dioxide from carbon sinks such as coal and oil?
questions.explanation: Combustion of biomass, peat, coal, oil and natural gas releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Compared with natural combustion, human activities have:
questions.explanation: Combustion sometimes happens naturally (e.g. lightning strikes), but human activities have greatly increased combustion rates.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: The Keeling Curve shows atmospheric carbon dioxide over time.
questions.stem: The long-term trend of the Keeling Curve is:

options.text: A rise in carbon dioxide concentration

questions.explanation: The Keeling Curve shows a long-term rise in atmospheric CO2, with annual fluctuations from seasonal photosynthesis.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: The Keeling Curve shows annual fluctuations in carbon dioxide.
questions.stem: These seasonal dips are mainly caused by:
questions.explanation: Annual fluctuations reflect seasonal changes in photosynthesis, respiration and combustion.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Aerobic respiration depends on atmospheric oxygen produced by:
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration depends on atmospheric oxygen from photosynthesis, while photosynthesis depends on CO2 from respiration.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Which statement about the recycling of elements is correct?

options.text: All elements used by living organisms are recycled, with decomposers playing a key role
Decomposers play no role in recycling

questions.explanation: All chemical elements required by living organisms are recycled in ecosystems, and decomposers play a key role.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: In a closed system, which can pass in and out?
questions.explanation: In a closed system only energy can pass in and out; ecosystems are open systems where both energy and matter move.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Chemical energy flows through a food chain from:

options.text: One organism to the consumer that feeds on it

questions.explanation: Chemical energy passes to a consumer as it feeds on the organism at the previous stage of the food chain.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Energy is required by autotrophs for:
questions.explanation: Autotrophs need energy for carbon fixation and for the anabolic reactions that build macromolecules.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Complex carbon compounds taken in by heterotrophs are first:

options.text: Digested, then assimilated into the compounds needed

questions.explanation: Heterotrophs digest complex compounds (externally or internally) and assimilate them by building the compounds they require.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: An organism that eats a secondary consumer is a:
questions.explanation: The trophic levels are producer, primary, secondary and tertiary consumer; a tertiary consumer eats a secondary consumer.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A student builds an energy pyramid from ecosystem data.
questions.stem: The pyramid represents:

options.text: Energy transfer and losses between trophic levels

questions.explanation: Energy pyramids use research data to represent energy transfer and losses between trophic levels in food chains.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Heat is produced both when ATP is made and when it is:
questions.explanation: Because energy transfers are inefficient, heat is produced both when ATP is produced and when it is used in cells.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: Although there is less biomass at higher trophic levels, the energy content per unit mass:
questions.stem: Is best described as:
questions.explanation: There is less biomass at higher levels, but the energy content per unit mass is not reduced.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: A food web differs from a food chain in that it:

options.text: Shows only one line of feeding

questions.explanation: A food web represents the many interconnected feeding relationships in a community, unlike a single food chain.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: A scientific law describes a pattern in nature.
questions.stem: How do laws differ from theories?

options.text: Laws describe phenomena but do not explain them

questions.explanation: Laws are generalized principles that describe patterns without explaining them; like theories, they can make predictions.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Photosynthesis depends on atmospheric carbon dioxide produced by:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis relies on CO2 from respiration, and respiration relies on O2 from photosynthesis - a major interaction between autotrophs and heterotrophs.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Which of these supplies energy to decomposers?
questions.explanation: Decomposers gain energy from carbon compounds in dead organic matter: faeces, dead parts and dead whole organisms.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Biomass accumulates in an ecosystem when organisms:
questions.explanation: Biomass accumulates as autotrophs and heterotrophs grow or reproduce; biomes vary in this capacity.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Secondary production is lower than primary production because heterotrophs:

options.text: Lose biomass as carbon compounds are respired to CO2 and water

questions.explanation: Loss of biomass through respiration means heterotroph (secondary) production is lower than autotroph (primary) production.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Carbon sinks such as coal, oil and peat differ in their:
questions.explanation: These carbon sinks vary in their date of formation; combustion of them releases stored carbon as CO2.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.stem: Why can matter be recycled in ecosystems but energy cannot?

options.text: Matter is reused, but energy is ultimately lost as heat
Energy is reused but matter is lost

questions.explanation: Chemical elements (matter) are recycled, but energy flows through and is ultimately lost as heat, so it must be constantly replaced.

questions.category: C4.2 Transfers of energy and matter
questions.scenario: In a carbon cycle diagram, carbon moves from the atmosphere into producers.
questions.stem: Which process achieves this?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis fixes atmospheric carbon dioxide into carbon compounds in producers, part of the carbon cycle.
